blocks.md: A **dfn** is a function written in braces. Inside, `⍵` (omega) is the right argument and `⍺` (alpha) is the left. The value of the last expression is the result.
Use a dfn with no `⍺` as a one-argument (monadic) function, and with `⍺` as a two-argument (dyadic) one. Give it a name with `←` and call it like any built-in.
Because the argument is an array, your function is automatically vectorised — `dbl` doubled the whole vector with no loop. This is the APL way: write the operation once, let it apply to everything.

blocks.prompt: Define a function sq that squares its argument, then square 12. Expect 144.
blocks.hint: result is the last expression; multiply ⍵ by itself

blocks.prompt: Define mean as sum divided by count, then take the mean of 10 20 30 40. Expect 25.
Define a dyadic function pct that returns ⍺ as a percentage of ⍵ (100 × ⍺ ÷ ⍵), then compute 30 pct 200. Expect 15.
blocks.hint: ⍺ is the left argument, ⍵ the right

blocks.prompt: Define a function that sums the first ⍵ integers, then apply it to 10. Expect 55.
